title: Pink Fleshy Growth on Your Dog's Eyelid: Triage Guide
meta_desc: Discover what a pink growth on your dog's eyelid might be, why it's usually low urgency, and how a photo helps your vet triage the issue.
content_html:
What is this growth?

Finding a new pink growth on your dog’s eyelid can be worrying, but it is a common occurrence in many dogs. These fleshy masses are often meibomian gland adenomas, which are benign (non-cancerous) tumors arising from the oil-producing glands in the eyelid. They typically appear as small, pink, or skin-colored lumps that may have a slightly bumpy, cauliflower-like texture.

Is it an emergency?

The urgency level for this condition is generally Low. While any new growth should be evaluated by a veterinarian, a pink eyelid mass is rarely an immediate medical emergency. You should schedule a routine appointment within the next week. However, if the growth is bleeding, causing the eye to appear cloudy, or if your dog is squinting and pawing at the eye, you should seek care sooner to prevent secondary irritation to the cornea.

Why a photo helps triage

Providing a clear, close-up photo of the growth is one of the most helpful things you can do for your veterinary team. High-quality images allow a professional to assess the exact location of the mass, its size, and whether it is rubbing against the surface of the eye. This visual information helps determine if the growth can be monitored or if surgical removal is necessary to prevent future discomfort or vision issues.

faqs.question: Can I use over-the-counter eye drops on the growth?
faqs.answer: No, you should avoid using any medications or drops without a veterinarian's recommendation, as some ingredients can be harmful if the surface of the eye is already irritated.

faqs.question: Do these eyelid growths ever go away on their own?
faqs.answer: Most eyelid tumors in dogs do not disappear on their own and tend to grow slowly over time. If they become large enough to irritate the eye, surgical removal is usually recommended.

faqs.question: Is my dog in pain from this growth?
faqs.answer: Most small pink eyelid growths are not painful themselves. However, if the growth is positioned in a way that it rubs against the cornea, it can cause significant irritation and discomfort.
